text: If ultraviolet radiation of 6.2 eV falls on an aluminium surface, then kinetic energy of the fastest emitted electron is (work-function = 4.2 eV)
explanation:
Energy of ultraviolet radiation, E = 6.2 eV.
Work-function, ϕ = 4.2 eV.
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, kinetic energy of the fastest emitted electron,

\( \frac{1}{2} m v_{\max}^2 = E - \phi = 6.2 - 4.2 = 2 \) eV

\( = 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \) J

\( \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} m v_{\max}^2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-19} \) J.
name: Questions Item 1

text: The retarding potential necessary to stop the emission of photoelectrons, when a target material of work function 1.24 eV is irradiated with light of wavelength 4.36 × 10^{-7} m is
explanation:
Given, work function, ϕ₀ = 1.24 eV.
Wavelength, λ = 4.36 × 10^{-7} m.
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,

\( eV_0 = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi_0 \)

\( \Rightarrow eV_0 = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4.36 \times 10^{-7}} - 1.24 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \)

\( V_0 = \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.36 \times 10^{-7}} - 1.24 \)

\( = 2.85 - 1.24 = 1.61 \) eV ≈ 1.60 eV.
name: Questions Item 2

text: Which of the following statements is correct regarding photoelectric effect?
options: The electrons are emitted if emitting surface is at high temperature.
Photoemission occurs if wavelength is less than a critical value.
The KE of photoelectrons is proportional to the square of the amplitude of incident radiation.
The photoelectric current is proportional to the frequency of incident radiation.
explanation: When the wavelength of incident radiation on the metal surface is less than threshold (critical) wavelength, then photoelectric emission takes place.
name: Questions Item 3

text: If the wavelength of a photon is 4000 Å, then its energy will be
explanation:
Wavelength of photon, λ = 4000 Å = 4 × 10^{-7} m.
Energy of photon, E = hc/λ

\( = \frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}} \)

\( = 4.96 \times 10^{-19} \) J = 4.95 × 10^{-19} J.
name: Questions Item 4

text: The maximum velocity of an electron emitted by light of wavelength λ incident on the surface of a metal of work function φ is [h = Planck's constant, m = mass of electron and c = speed of light]
explanation:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is

\( K_{\text{max}} = \frac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}m v_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{hc - \lambda\phi}{\lambda} \)

\( \Rightarrow v_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{2(hc - \lambda\phi)}{m\lambda} \Rightarrow v_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(hc - \lambda\phi)}{m\lambda}} \).
name: Questions Item 5

text: A metal surface is illuminated by a light of given intensity and frequency to cause photoemission. If the intensity of illumination is reduced to one-fourth of its original value, then the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons would become
options: four times of the original value
explanation: In photoelectric emission, maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons does not depend upon the intensity of incident radiation. It depends upon the frequency of incident radiation. Hence, when intensity of incident radiation is reduced to one-fourth, then maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons remains unchanged.
name: Questions Item 6

text: In a photoelectric experiment, a monochromatic light is incident on the emitter plate E, as shown in the figure. When switch S₁ is closed and switch S₂ is open, the photoelectrons strike the collector plate C with a maximum kinetic energy of 1 eV. If switch S₁ is open and switch S₂ is closed and the frequency of the incident light is doubled the photoelectrons strike the collector plate with a maximum kinetic energy of 20 eV. The threshold wavelength of the emitter plate is
explanation:
Let threshold frequency of emitter plate = ν₀.
Energy of photon in first case is E.
When switch S₁ is closed and switch S₂ is open,

\( E = h\nu_0 + (5 + 1) \) eV ... (i)

For second case, when switch S₁ is open and switch S₂ is closed and frequency of incident light is doubled.

\( 2E = h\nu_0 + (20 - 5) \) eV ... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

\( \Rightarrow 2(h\nu_0 + 6\text{eV}) = h\nu_0 + 15\text{eV} \)

\( \Rightarrow 2h\nu_0 + 12\text{eV} = h\nu_0 + 15\text{eV} \)

\( \Rightarrow h\nu_0 = 3\text{eV} \)

\( \Rightarrow \nu_0 = \frac{3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.62 \times 10^{-34}} = 7.25 \times 10^{14} \) Hz

\( \therefore \lambda_0 = \frac{c}{\nu_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{7.25 \times 10^{14}} \)

\( \lambda_0 = 4133.3 \) Å.
name: Questions Item 7

text: An electron of charge e and mass m moving with an initial velocity v₀ î is subjected to all electric field E₀ ĵ. The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron at a time t is (Initial de-Broglie wavelength of the electron = λ₀)
explanation:
de-Broglie relation of a charged particles,

\( \lambda = \frac{h}{mv} \)

Velocity of charged particle at time t,

\( \mathbf{v} = v_0 \hat{\mathbf{i}} + \frac{eE_0}{m} t \hat{\mathbf{j}} \)

\( |\mathbf{v}| = \sqrt{v_0^2 + \left(\frac{eE_0}{m} t\right)^2} \)

Hence, \( \lambda = \frac{h}{m\sqrt{v_0^2 + \left(\frac{eE_0}{m} t\right)^2}} \)

\( \lambda = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{e^2E_0^2 t^2}{m^2 v_0^2}}} \).
name: Questions Item 8

text: The maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron liberated from the surface of lithium with work function 2.35 eV by electromagnetic radiation whose electric component varies with time as : E = a[1 + cos(2π f₁ t)] cos(2π f₂ t) (where a is a constant) is (f₁ = 3.6 × 10¹⁵ Hz, and f₂ = 1.2 × 10¹⁵ Hz and Planck's constant h = 6.6 × 10^{-34} Js)
explanation:
Here, work function, W₀ = 2.35 eV and the electric component of electromagnetic radiation E = a[1 + cos(2π f₁ t)] cos(2π f₂ t).

\( \Rightarrow E = a \cos(2\pi f_2 t) + a \cos(2\pi f_1 t) \cos(2\pi f_2 t) \)

\( \therefore \cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2} [\cos(A + B) - \cos(A - B)] \)

\( \Rightarrow E = a \cos(2\pi f_2 t) + \frac{a}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_1 + f_2) t - \frac{a}{2} \cos 2\pi(f_1 - f_2) t \)

So, the electric component has 3 sub-components with frequencies are,

f₂, (f₁ + f₂) and (f₁ - f₂).

So, for maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron, we take photon of maximum frequency. Hence,

\( E_{\text{max}} = \frac{h\nu_{\text{max}}}{e} \)

\( = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times (3.6 \times 10^{15} + 1.2 \times 10^{15})}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \)

\( = 19.8 \) eV.

Hence, the maximum kinetic energy,

\( KE_{\text{max}} = E_{\text{max}} - W_0 = 19.8 - 2.35 = 17.45 \) eV.
name: Questions Item 9

text: An α-particle moves in a circular path of radius 1 cm in a uniform magnetic field of 0.125 T. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the α-particle is
explanation:
Given magnetic field, B = 0.125 T,
Radius of the circular path, R = 1 cm = 10^{-2} m.
We know radius of circular path in a uniform magnetic field,

\( R = \frac{mv}{qB} \Rightarrow mv = qBR \)

and de-Broglie wavelength, λ = h/(mv)

\( \therefore q = q_\alpha \) for α-particle.

\( \lambda = \frac{h}{q_\alpha BR} \)

\( \lambda = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.125 \times 1 \times 10^{-2}} \)

\( \Rightarrow \lambda = 1.65 \times 10^{-12} \) m.
name: Questions Item 10

text: All electrons ejected from a metallic surface by incident light of wavelength 400 nm travelled 1 m in the direction of uniform electric field of 2 NC^{-1} and came to rest. The work function of the surface is
explanation:
Given, wavelength of incident radiation, λ = 400 nm = 4 × 10^{-7} m,
electric field, E = 2 N/C and distance, s = 1 m.
Energy of the incident light,

\( E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}} \)

\( = 4.95 \times 10^{-19} \) J = 3.09 eV ≈ 3.1 eV.

If a be the retardation of emitted electrons in the electric field,

\( a = \frac{qE}{m} \)

∴ Maximum kinetic energy of the electron,

\( KE_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{2} m u^2 = m \cdot \frac{2qE}{m} = qE = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2 \)

\( KE_{\text{max}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-19} \) J = 2 eV.

∴ Work function of the surface,

\( W_0 = E - KE_{\text{max}} = 3.1 - 2 = 1.1 \) eV.
name: Questions Item 11

text: All electrons ejected from a metal surface by the incident light of wavelength 200 nm can be stopped before travelling 1 m in the direction of uniform electric field of 4 NC^{-1}. The work function of the metal surface is
explanation:
Given, wavelength, λ = 200 nm, electric field, E = 4 NC^{-1} and distance, d = 1 m.
Since, electron is stopped in distance d in the direction of electric field E, then the stopping potential is given as,

\( V_s = E \cdot d = 4 \times 1 = 4 \) V.

Energy of photon,

\( E = \frac{1250}{\lambda_{\text{nm}}} \) eV

\( \Rightarrow E = \frac{1250}{200} = 6.2 \) eV.

Einstein's photoelectric equation,

\( E = W_0 + KE \)

\( \Rightarrow W_0 = E - eV \)

because KE_max = stopping potential

\( \Rightarrow W_0 = 6.2 - 4 = 2.2 \) eV.
name: Questions Item 12

text: Light of wavelength 488 nm produced by an Argon laser is used in the photoelectric effect. When light from this spectral line is incident on the cathode, the stopping potential of the photoelectrons is 0.38 V. The work function of the cathode material is
explanation:
Given, light of wavelength, λ = 488 nm and stopping potential, of photoelectrons, V = 0.38 V.
As, energy of photon

\( E = \frac{1250}{\lambda} \) eV

So,

\( E = \frac{1250}{488} = 2.56 \) eV.

∴ Maximum kinetic energy of a photo-electron

\( KE_{\text{max}} = eV_0 \)

Where, V₀ = stopping potential

\( \therefore KE_{\text{max}} = eV_0 = 0.38 \) eV.

From Einstein's photoelectric equation,

\( E = KE_{\text{max}} + W_0 \)

\( \Rightarrow W_0 = E - KE_{\text{max}} \)

\( = 2.56 - 0.38 = 2.18 \approx 2.16 \) eV.
name: Questions Item 13

text: The figures shows the variation of photo-current i with anode potential V for three differential radiations. Let I_a, I_b and I_c be the intensities and f_a, f_b and f_c be the frequencies for the curves a, b and c respectively. Then
explanation:
Stopping potentials for b and a are same

∴ f_a = f_b

and saturation current for b and a are different

∴ I_a ≠ I_b.
name: Questions Item 14

text: An α-particle and a proton are accelerated from rest by the same potential, then the ratio of their de-Broglie wavelength is
name: Questions Item 15
